questions.prompt: What is the main reason the lesson says identity must be rebuilt for change to last?
choices: Because real change requires a new set of rules to follow consistently
Because identity shapes behavior, and old identity quietly pulls you back under pressure
Because you need a clean reputation before you can live differently
Because once your circumstances improve, your identity will naturally improve

questions.prompt: The lesson is careful to say “new identity in Christ” does NOT mean:
choices: You ignore consequences and pretend your past never happened
You stop being defined by old labels at the deepest level
You learn to live from what God says is true
You make choices that fit who you are becoming

questions.prompt: According to the lesson’s explanation of 2 Corinthians 5:17, being “a new creature” is best understood as:
choices: A gradual self-improvement project that becomes true after enough good behavior
A new spiritual reality God creates in a person who is in Christ
A new public image that helps you earn trust faster
A stronger personality that makes temptation easier to resist

questions.prompt: In the lesson, “If any man…” is emphasized to show:
choices: Only people with a clean record can claim new life in Christ
This promise is mainly for people who grew up in church
No category of sinner is excluded from the promise if he is in Christ
The verse is aimed at spiritual leaders more than ordinary believers

questions.prompt: Which statement best reflects how the lesson contrasts “in Christ” with “trying harder”?
choices: In Christ means God gives a new identity that does not depend on your performance
In Christ means you feel new most days if you stay disciplined
In Christ means you avoid all old triggers and environments
In Christ means you become morally superior to your past

questions.prompt: When the lesson says “old things are passed away,” the best interpretation is:
choices: Your legal record is erased and relationships instantly reset
Old patterns and labels no longer define who you truly are in God’s sight
Your memories disappear so you don’t feel shame anymore
Your temptations stop because the old nature is gone completely

questions.prompt: The lesson’s point about “Behold, all things are become new” is mainly that:
choices: Every circumstance changes immediately if your faith is strong enough
Your deepest standing and identity before God are new, even if circumstances lag
God gives new opportunities first, then identity follows
New identity is mostly a mindset technique to stay positive

questions.prompt: Which idea best captures the lesson’s definition of shame-based identity?
choices: Shame says, “I did wrong,” and motivates improvement
Shame says, “I am wrong,” and pushes a person to live down to his worst labels
Shame is mainly a sign you’re becoming spiritually mature
Shame is useful because it keeps people from making mistakes

questions.prompt: The lesson teaches that social labels like “felon” or “addict” are dangerous mainly because:
choices: They are always inaccurate and should never be used
They can sink into self-concept and shape decisions: “this is what guys like me do”
They are illegal for anyone to say once you’re out
They prove society is the main cause of sin

questions.prompt: Psychology can encourage “rewrite your story,” but the lesson says it cannot provide an unshakable identity because:
choices: It usually anchors identity in self-will, achievement, or desire—things that can fail or be taken
It discourages responsibility and makes people blame their past
It focuses only on feelings and ignores actions
It denies that identity affects behavior

questions.prompt: The “integration” section agrees with psychology on what key point?
choices: Identity shapes behavior, so changing identity changes choices
People can heal without community if they are disciplined enough
Shame is the best tool for long-term growth
The past must be forgotten or healing can’t happen

questions.prompt: Which response best matches the lesson’s recommended way to fight a shame-lie like “You’re still the same”?
choices: Ignore the thought and distract yourself until it fades
Argue with people so they stop reminding you of your past
Answer with Scripture-truth: old things passed away; I will not agree with that lie
Promise yourself you will never fail again

questions.prompt: The lesson says the Christian life is not working your way into being new, but:
choices: Working out what is already true because God has made you new in Christ
Working until you feel new, then you can act new
Working to prove to people you’ve changed so God will accept you
Working to erase consequences so the past no longer matters

questions.prompt: Which practical choice best fits “living from new identity” in the house?
choices: Doing chores only when someone is watching so you look consistent
Serving without being asked because you belong to Christ who served you
Avoiding responsibility so you don’t risk failure
Keeping your struggles private so you appear strong

questions.prompt: The lesson’s phrase “not a repaired version of the old you” is mainly teaching that God is:
choices: Mostly improving your circumstances so you can restart life
Creating a new spiritual reality, not just polishing the old man
Helping you build a better public image than before
Making you immune to temptation once you mature

questions.prompt: The “In Christ Confession” practice is described as:
choices: Positive thinking that works if you say it with enough energy
Agreeing out loud with what God has said, even when feelings disagree
A ritual that replaces repentance and daily obedience
A way to prove your faith to others

questions.prompt: The lesson warns that if you still believe “I am what I did,” pressure will likely produce:
choices: More confidence because you know your limits
A drift back into the life that fits that belief
A desire to serve others to make up for the past
A deeper interest in theology and study

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the closing encouragement of Week 10?
choices: Your identity rests on Christ’s finished work; when you fail, return to the cross and keep building
Once you claim a new identity, you should never feel like the old man again
Your identity becomes secure when others fully trust you
The program itself is what makes you new

questions.prompt: The lesson’s main claim about fellowship is that it is:
choices: An optional activity for believers who are extra serious
A core part of what it means to be joined to Christ and His people
Helpful mainly for networking and job opportunities
A substitute for personal prayer and Bible reading

questions.prompt: Romans 12:4–5 is used to teach that believers are:
choices: One body in Christ and members one of another
One body because they share similar backgrounds and values
One body if they attend the same meetings consistently
One body only after they prove long-term stability

questions.prompt: In the lesson, “members one of another” means:
choices: We should all have the same opinions to stay united
We belong to each other in a real spiritual way; what we do affects the body
We should keep relationships shallow to avoid conflict
We are responsible to control each other’s decisions

questions.prompt: 1 Corinthians 12 emphasizes that unity in the church is created primarily by:
choices: Shared preferences and shared lifestyles
Strong leadership and strict rules
One Spirit placing believers into one body
Consistent meeting attendance over time

questions.prompt: The lesson uses the body picture to correct which mindset most directly?
choices: Both inferiority (“I don’t matter”) and independence (“I don’t need you”)
Only laziness and lack of motivation
Only doctrinal confusion about salvation
Only fear of speaking in public

questions.prompt: Which statement best matches the lesson’s point about diversity in the body?
choices: Differences are a problem, so unity requires sameness
Differences exist by God’s design; the body needs many kinds of members to function
Differences are acceptable as long as nobody serves publicly
Differences matter mostly for personality, not spiritual life

questions.prompt: When the lesson says “the eye cannot say to the hand, I have no need of thee,” it is mainly confronting:
choices: Comparison that produces shame and withdrawal
Pride that produces independence and dismissal of others
Fear that produces silence in group settings
Immaturity that produces too many questions

questions.prompt: According to the lesson, the “feeble” members being “necessary” teaches that:
choices: Weakness is proof someone should step back from involvement
Every member matters; even those who seem small or weak have a necessary place
Only gifted members should be visible to keep the body strong
People with rough pasts should stay on the edges until they feel ready

questions.prompt: The lesson describes real fellowship most closely as:
choices: Shared burdens and shared joys—suffering together and rejoicing together
Attending the same services and keeping conversations polite
Agreeing to avoid hard topics so peace is maintained
Helping only when asked so boundaries stay clear

questions.prompt: Psychologically, the lesson says men often struggle with fellowship because they learned:
choices: Trust is dangerous; caring leads to hurt, so distance feels safer
Group life is too boring to maintain long-term
Faith is mainly private and shouldn’t be shared
Accountability is unnecessary once a man prays

questions.prompt: From a recovery perspective, the lesson says community helps change largely because it provides:
choices: A place to be honest without being alone, accountability, examples, and opportunities to help others
A quick fix that eliminates temptation through social pressure
A way to earn acceptance from God through participation
A substitute for personal responsibility

questions.prompt: The lesson describes “hyper-independence” (I don’t need anybody) as:
choices: A mature sign that a man is ready to be released early
A survival strategy that increases relapse risk by cutting off healthy support
A spiritual discipline that protects a man from bad influences
A personality type that can’t be changed

questions.prompt: In the integration section, theology strengthens the community idea by teaching that:
choices: God saves individuals but leaves community as an optional add-on
Belonging is rooted in Christ and the Spirit placing you into His body, not just social preference
Community works mainly because it increases motivation and self-esteem
The best fellowship is avoiding people who challenge you

questions.prompt: The lesson says the program’s structure (shared living, group, worship, mentorship) is best understood as:
choices: A set of add-ons that help if you personally enjoy them
A strategy to keep men busy so they don’t relapse
An expression of a biblical reality: learning to live as members of one body
A replacement for personal conviction and prayer

questions.prompt: When fellowship exposes conflict and “rough edges,” the lesson’s best framing is:
choices: Proof you don’t belong, so you should pull back to protect yourself
A training ground where the new man learns to listen, apologize, forgive, and work through issues
A sign leadership is failing and rules need to tighten
Evidence that community is too risky for most people

questions.prompt: Which daily action most fits the lesson’s “One-Another Practice”?
choices: Pick one brother daily and practice a simple one-another act: encourage, pray, bear burdens, forgive, or serve
Try to fix everyone’s problems so the house runs smoothly
Avoid weaker members so you don’t get pulled down
Stay quiet and focus only on your own growth this week

questions.prompt: The lesson’s most direct warning about living the Christian life as a “solo project” is that:
choices: You will likely drift back into old ways of thinking and living
You will lose your salvation over time
You will become too dependent on others to mature
You will stop needing discipline and structure

questions.prompt: Which statement best matches the closing pastoral encouragement of Week 11?
choices: Real fellowship is too risky, so keep relationships minimal and safe
God saved you into a body; don’t let conflict push you back into isolation—risk belonging under Christ
The goal is to become independent enough that community no longer matters
The body works best when only leaders carry burdens
